sentences.text: My name is Alexander B.-- which stands for Bialywlos, my old name, B-I-A-L-Y-W-L-O-S-- White, which is my present name, W-H-I-T-E.
My birth date is June 4, 1923.
I didn't hear that.
I was born in a small town called Krosno, which is right near the Dukla Pass of the Carpathian Mountains in southern Poland.
Well, it was a small town.
It had approximately 15,000 to 16,000 population.
There were a few thousand Jews living in that town.
And it was very much like any shtetl in the old country-- a picture of which I believe you have, if you want to show it later on.
And the Jews did very much what they used to do in the shtetls.
Many were tradespeople, carpenters, bakers, glaziers, blacksmiths, et cetera.
And some had stores.
And they lived amongst the non-Jewish population quite peacefully, and went on to live their lives the way they had for years and years prior to that.
The members of my family, of course, my parents.
My father's name was a Mendel.
And my mother's name was Leah.
My mother's maiden name was Platnar.
And of course, I had my grandfather, my grandmother, on my mother's side.
We lived together in the same little apartment, which consisted really of two rooms and a storefront in front of it.
And of course, I had two brothers-- one, one year younger than myself, and one was born the 1929, and one sister who was one year older than myself.
My sister's name was Miriam.
We call her [?
Manya. ?] My brother's name Schlomo.
We called him [?
Shlomeck. ?] And my little brother's name was the Henek, which actually his Hebrew name was Chaim Hirsch, because that was my grandfather's name, after whom he was named.
On my mother's side.
The grandfather of my-- of my father's side was [?
Indom ?] [?
Brova, ?] in a small town where my father comes from, near the city of Tarnow, which is not far from Krakow.
And my grandmother on my father's side had been killed during World War I by a shrapnel which hit her as she was hiding on the cemetery when they were attacking the town, the-- the Russians.
My father was in the glazing business.
He was a glazier.
Plus, we had, together with an uncle of mine, a retail as well as a wholesale plate glass business.
And we also had a-- a-- a glass and mirror-making establishment.
And we made to also putty and such other things related to glass framing as well.
Yes.
We-- the retail was in that building.
But we had a warehouse at the train station, so that trains could unload right then and there.
So the wholesale business was there.
Well, you know, as a youngster, since my family was a Orthodox family, you know, at the age of three I started what's called the cheder, which is a school.
And we ran around barefoot for the most part in the summer.
We went to the cheder early in the morning, all afternoon, just about the entire day.
We were picked up-- the children were picked up-- by what was called the [?
Bellefaire ?] which is a helper to the-- to the teacher.
And we used to go there.
We studied, you know, the Bible and et cetera.
And we played there.
And then, as I came of age to go to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] school, of course-- most Jewish kids or many did not go to a secular school.
But my father, though he was very Orthodox, he was very learned.
And he insisted that we get a secular education.
So we went to regular school.
And as I got older-- I started at the age of six-- although school started at the age of seven.
The reason what is this is, we spoke Yiddish at home.
My father thought, well, I have to learn Polish first, or I won't pass.
So they put me in there at the age of six, because they knew with the director of the school.
He used to come to our store.
So they petitioned him to allow me to go there since I would probably stay over an extra year.
So I started at the age of six.
But I surprised them, because I passed right the first year, that I was really the youngest in the school.
And later on as I got older, there was a yeshiva.
So I, of course, went there.
And I used to get up at about 5:30 in the morning, so as to be at 6 o'clock in the synagogue where the yeshiva was.
And I was there until about 7:30 studying.

And at 7:30, I went home, changed my attire, you know, since to the Jewish school I wore what you see sometimes that Orthodox Jews wore, those round little hats, you know, and the long kaftan, you know.
And I changed rapidly, put my little [INAUDIBLE] that I had behind my ear, and went-- went to a regular school, which was right on our block.
So it only took me a few minutes.
And there I was usually until about 1 o'clock sometimes 2 o'clock.
And then, I went home, did my homework.
Then, I went back to the Jewish school.
And in the winter, you know, you took your lantern.
And you went to school in the evening, the Jewish school.
And that's how life went on.
And at times, I helped out at home.
Even at the age of nine and 10, I helped out in the store.
I would carry, for example, glass wherever you had to carry it to.
As a matter of fact, I was even manufacturing putty, believe it or not.
I was an expert in mixing it and making it of the proper consistency, better than the fellow who used to work for us.
And having had a steady hand, for example, if we had to glaze large windows, like the windows here, since I had a very steady hand, I would get on the ladder.
And I would cut straight, a straight line, without having to use a lineal.
So lots of times, I used to do all of these things.
I used to help out making mirrors.
So we were busy all day long.
My father was a very hard working man, and he used to get up very early, at about the same time that I got up 5:00, 6 o'clock.
And he worked usually until midnight, and sometimes even longer.
And he also kept the books.
Our business was combined, my father and my uncle.
My uncle owned the majority of shares in the business, because it was his father's.
In other words, my mother's brother's parents, my grandparents, had owned it.
And he was the main man there.
And my father was taken in after he married my mother.
But he was to do all the bookkeeping, and so on.
So he would be up sometime 2 o'clock, 3 o'clock.
And he used to do the bookkeeping chanting.
That's how I learned chanting, you know, one and one is two, and two and two is four.
And he would, you know, do the bookkeeping.
So my grandmother, my grandfather, when they were still alive, on Thursday they started baking for the Sabbath.
And since we are all, more or less, were in one of two rooms, you know, I mean the smell of that stuff was absolutely fantastic.
And then my uncle had a huge tree, a pear tree-- huge one.
So we used to pick up those pears and dry them in the oven.
It was a very, as you would call, heimish and-- and Jewish.
And it was a very pleasant life.
And of course, we had a river in our town.
The Wislok River was the name of it.
So we used to go swimming.
You didn't take lessons of swimming.
You learned swimming on your own when you almost drowned, then you knew how to swim.
Anyway, that's how the days went on, day after day.
Yes, a few of them.
My cousin-- I have a first cousin who now lives in Manchester.
He survived when he joined the Polish army in exile.
It's called the Anders' Army, and he fought in the Middle East.
He was at North Africa and then in Italy.
And he how-- then married a girl from Poland who was studying in England.
And when he was demobilized in England, he just married there and stayed there.
Now, he was with me in school.
And there were about two or three others.
There were 40 some kids in the class.
I had friends that were not Jewish, but Jewish kids, especially like myself from an Orthodox family, did not really fraternize with gentile kids.
Now, my cousin was a different story.
Although they were Orthodox too, the kids were rebellious.
And he was a rebel.
And he had friends among the gentile kids.

And he would, as a matter of fact, gang up on kids, who, for example, on the Sabbath when the Jews used to come back from the synagogue, and some kids would throw stones at them, he would gang up and hide.
And if he caught these kids, would beat the heck out of them.
And he had gentile kids that were with him.
They were his buddy buddies, so they would all-- he would remember who it was who threw the stones.
And they would gang up, and then, beat that kid up awfully.
He was a rebel, and even is a rebel now.
His own kids tell the story that he walks around with a gun.
He has a gun at home.
And he is a member of the gun club.
You know, and he has also gentile friends who are gun club members.
But he was a different sort of a story than myself.
I was religious.
And although they were religious as well, they would, for example, hide and buy some non-kosher sausages or send in a friend to buy them non-kosher sausages.
Or they would smoke on the Sabbath, you know, hide somewhere so that no one will see them.
And that wasn't the case with our family.
Mark Frooman.
He lives in Manchester.
He was just in New York to a wedding.
And we had met there again.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as comfortable as you could.
There were some kids, you know, who were, you know, anti-Semitic.
You know, they learned it at home.
And for example, I would be roughed up sometimes outside.
I remember one episode in winter I went home.
They waited for me outside, threw me into the snow, rubbed me up was snow, my face was all swollen.
And my mother went to the teacher to talk to him.
His name was Grouchka-- a fine gentleman.
I must say the teachers were wonderful, absolutely wonderful.
And he grabbed that kid who did that to me, and he called him out in class and bawled the heck out of him, telling him his father used to work for a Jewish company.
There was a rubber, huge plant called Wudeta-- stood for Wurzel, Daar, Tarnow-- because they were the owners from the city of Tarnow.
And he told him, you eat Jewish bread, for God's sake, and this is what you did to him?
He bawled the heck out of him.
So all for example to tell you quite frankly, I sometimes don't like to tell those stories, because some of my Polish friends, you know, don't like when I talk that way.
But what I'm saying is the truth.
If I had to go to the washroom, I went home to go to the washroom rather than go to the washroom when all the other kids.
Or I would ask for permission during the lecture to go, so that no other kids were there.
Because they would turn around occasionally, and they would pee on me, you see?
So I-- I either ran home, or I went-- or I went during the hour of schooling to the washroom.
So you know, but I guess you learn to live with these things.
And you survive.
Did your father give you advice about how to deal with such incidences?
Not really.
Not really.
Well, you know, in-- in the store, in the business, you really did not see it, you know.
But you saw it on the streets, because there were slogans, you know, and graffiti all over the place.
Don't buy from Jews.
[SPEAKING POLISH] that means, go to your own for what you need.
[SPEAKING POLISH] that means Jews to Palestine, you know, things of that sort.
And there were the [INAUDIBLE], which were nationalists, Polish nationalists.
And they would gang up, for example, and you know, break the windows in the Jewish sports club.
Or for example, they would boycott Jewish stores.
They never did our store, for one reason or another.
But for example, at-- when school started, and there was a big store that sold school items-- [?
Hertsig ?] was his name.
And they would, for example, go and boycott it and not allow Poles to go into the store.
Or before the holidays before the Christmas, et cetera.
And that all started after Pilsudski, Marshal Pilsudski, who was the leader of Poland, that was a sort of a quasi-dictator died in 1935.
And then, the nationalists-- you know, nationalist feeling came to the fore when Hitler took over, you see.
And when he died, there was no one to dampen their enthusiasm, you know, to hate Jews.
And that's when all of that started.
Before that, you didn't see it.
As a matter of fact, when he died, the Jews cried.
I remember my mother was shedding tears when he died.
So from that time on, Smigly-Rydz took over.
He was a general.
And he was a nationalist.
And that's when really anti-Semitism really got to the surface.
A number of examples I can give you.
For example, I went glazing with my uncle one time.
I was carrying glass.
He was carrying glass.
And there was a fellow from the brick factory on a-- on a with a horse and a buggy, and so on.
And as we were walking aside from the road, he took his whip, and he hit my uncle a number of times.
He fell with the glass, broke the glass, and so on.
Another instance, for example, my father was going to glaze in a refinery.
So they came with a bus to pick us up from the refinery.
And I went to help my father.
And the driver, for example, would drive, all of a sudden he would turn sharply rather, so that we would fall with the glass.
I was cut up.
My father was cut up.
The glass was all broken.
He did it, you know, specifically to hurt us.
Well, I mean, instances like this.
Now, I remember when my uncle was hit.
He was a good friend of the mayor in our town, Mr.
Bergman.
And he went to the mayor.
And the mayor went with him to the [?
Starastra ?] which is the-- the federal in charge of the county.
And they called him.
And they gave him a day's jail.
But that was it.
Now, but these are just a few instances to show you.
But there were others, I have to tell you, I mean, that were extremely friendly.
For example, that teacher Grouchka, used to go to the schvitz, to the Jewish schvitz.
He used to love that.
He used to love Jews.
You know, the teachers were wonderful.
I-- I cannot complain.
They were really fine.
You know, it was part-- it was part of life.
I mean, it was part of your everyday life.
You just didn't discuss it, you know?
Well, prior to World War II, as the winds of war came in, my father and my uncle was sat down.
And I remember that distinctly, and were discussing leaving Poland, and going somewheres else.
Some Jews went to Palestine, but it was very difficult to go to Palestine.
They discussed it.
And they thought of building a glass factory in Palestine.
You know, they were well-to-do in our town.
As a matter of fact, the largest building in our town was ours, my father's and my uncle's.
My father had a smaller part in it.
And so we were considered wealthy.
I mean, I used to call it kishka money, that means, if you didn't eat, you're saving money.
But to everybody else, we were supposedly very wealthy.
And they were discussing going to Israel-- and they know Israel didn't have a factory, a glass factory-- to build it.
But I remember my uncle, my father said, well, it's so hot in Israel.
And in those days, when you make glass, you had to you know, weave it like this to cool it.
And it's too hot there.
So they decided not to go there.
Then, they talked about Mexico, to open a factory in Mexico.
And that also wasn't feasible.
So then they spoke about the United States.
And during the World Exhibit prior to World War II in New York, they decided that my uncle will go to New York.
And he will survey the area-- he had relatives in the United States-- and see whether we all couldn't go there.
But then, my father objected to it, because he said, look, here we are considered wealthy.
If we go to New York to the United States, we are going to be paupers.
We just as well stay here.
And they stayed there.
Joseph, Joseph Platnar.
He was my mother's brother.
Well, we just stayed there until World War II started.
When the war started in September, I was appointed prior to it, in school, you know.
I was trained to be a warden on that street.
And I was also trained to wear a gas mask.
There weren't enough gas masks, but since I was more educated and--
The school, the Polish school, yeah.
So I trained, you know, putting on a gas mask and go into a gas chamber.
I was trained with-- with a carabin, and all that, within the Polish school.
And so when the war broke out, it was around 2:00 or 3:00 in the morning.
There was a military air base in our town.
And all of a sudden, I heard the bombing.

I rushed out fast to the street to force people to go down into their cellars, put on a little mask that they showed us how to make out of gauze, wet it, and put it around your nose, and chased the people down into the cellars.
And there was lots of flak coming from the air base, and a few of the German planes were shot down.
The German [? planes, ?] where the [SPEAKING POLISH] which were dive bombers coming down, screaming down.
It was something awful.
We watched it from the outside.
People didn't want to go down into the basements.
They just watched the show, because this was a little bit out of town.
And they destroyed that air base that morning.
So that was that morning.
Now, of course, we had to have our windows darkened.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] People stopped going to the synagogue.
And if they went to prayers in the neighborhood, to somebody's house.
And initially, people thought that that war is only going to last a few days.
I remember when my parents and friends were discussing this.
They said, well, it's going to be a different war than World War I.
We now have, you know, airplanes and gas.
That war is going to be over within a few days.
No one dreamed-- dreamt that that's going to last four years.
But-- and initially, we thought that the Polish army is probably going to push the Nazis back.
But that was not to be.
The Polish army was completely unprepared for that war.
So a few days later, we found out that the Germans were coming closer to our town.
So we started running.
We hired a buggy, because we were too late for the last train.
My uncle took the last train with his family eastward.
We missed that train.
We took a horse buggy and moved out of town going east.
My uncle Haim did not want to go-- one of my uncles.
He said, what will the Germans do to me?
I know Germans from World War I.
They are not going to marinate me.
That were his words.
He said, he's staying.
So he stayed with his wife.
All his kids-- he had eight kids-- left.
And my uncle Abraham stayed.
His kids left.
And we finally came to a small town called Dynow, where we were stuck.
How do you spell the name of the town?
D-Y-N-O-W.
